severityMarkers: MODERATE: F4 over-translation on Arthas assessment
MODERATE: F7 closure sentence at chapter handoff

findings.line: Uther looked at Jaina then, properly, perhaps for the first time since the tent.
findings.reason: The hedge word 'perhaps' softens a clean physical read; deleting it keeps the same scene fact with more force.
findings.suggestedFix: Uther looked at Jaina then, properly, for the first time since the tent.

findings.line: Everything about him turned bad news into motion.
findings.reason: The previous sentence already shows Arthas converting information into action by measuring routes, bodies, and officer movement; this sentence translates what the concrete beat already carried.

findings.line: Up close the town's survival looked worse.
findings.reason: This is a thesis-opening sentence for the paragraph; the concrete burns, bites, barrels, and wood stacks already do the work.
The sentence announces the paragraph's subject before the stable boys, quartermasters, and relay strips show it directly.

findings.line: They rode for the head start while there was still one to ride for.
findings.reason: The chapter could end cleanly on the concrete image of horses, dust, and smoke; this line closes the emotional loop by explaining the urgency.

severityMarkers: Dedicated P5/P6 sweep found one new pace abstraction in the launch paragraph

findings.line: He led them between the tents at a pace that said he had already chosen what to leave undone for the next five minutes.
findings.reason: The non-agentive noun 'pace' is made to speak. The real agent is Uther's hurried movement and the triage work he is consciously leaving behind.
findings.suggestedFix: He led them between the tents fast enough to show what he had already chosen to leave undone for the next five minutes.

findings.line: Scholomance pressed at the edge of memory and stayed there.
findings.reason: A location name is given physical pressure and persistence in Sylvanas's memory. The real agent is the memory of what happened there and her refusal to dwell on it.
findings.suggestedFix: The memory of Scholomance stayed at the edge of her mind.

findings.line: Arthas's pace reached ahead of him.
findings.reason: The abstract noun 'pace' is doing physical advance work on its own. The real agents are the officers and column already moving under Arthas's urgency.
findings.suggestedFix: Mounted officers were already moving before Arthas reached the line.

audits.dominantPattern: B1 — sentence too polished for POV

findings.line: He made endurance look like an ordinary task someone had to do well.
findings.reason: This sentence reads like polished authorial compression rather than Sylvanas's clipped tactical register; it abstracts Uther into a theme statement after the concrete image already landed.

severityMarkers: Pattern 2 fragment pivots repeat in narration

findings.reason: The negative fragment is scaffolding. The paragraph already commits to the stronger positive description in the next sentence.

findings.line: Dry enough to be almost funny. Not meant that way.
findings.reason: The line defines Uther's tone through a negated second beat instead of naming the actual quality directly.

findings.line: Not enough to stop for. Too much to ignore.
findings.reason: The first fragment is a negated setup. The second fragment already carries the needed decision pressure on its own.

severityMarkers: Tic 5 cap is 4 per chapter; 8 eye/gaze beats were examined

findings.line: His eyes went from Sylvanas to Jaina to Uther and sharpened.
findings.reason: This is a generic reaction beat routed through eyes; it is one of the weakest excess eye/gaze instances once the chapter passes the hard cap.
findings.suggestedFix: He checked Sylvanas, Jaina, and Uther in one sweep.

findings.reason: Once the chapter exceeds the cap, this generic gaze beat is weaker than the more tactical or relationship-specific eye uses later in the scene.

findings.line: Jaina turned her head and gave him one clean look.
findings.reason: This is a generic confrontation beat carried by a look rather than a more specific action or line-level pressure.

findings.line: Uther looked at Jaina then, properly, perhaps for the first time since the tent.
findings.reason: After the chapter passes the cap, this relation beat is another weak eye/look construction and also duplicates the hedge problem already present in the same sentence.
findings.suggestedFix: Uther turned to Jaina then, properly, for the first time since the tent.

summary.verdictReason: 16 KILL verdicts across 5 guides (5 MODERATE+, 11 MILD). Recurring eye/gaze excess, fragment-negation pivots, and abstracted agency remain in the chapter.
